summaryHtml:
This memorial lecture by A. W. Downie traces the history of pneumococcal transformation, from Fred Griffith's seminal 1928 discoveries through Oswald Avery's landmark work identifying DNA as the transforming principle. Griffith's interest in bacterial typing for epidemiological purposes led to his surprising observation that rough, avirulent pneumococci could be transformed into virulent smooth forms when exposed to heat-killed virulent cells. Working under austere conditions at Britain's Ministry of Health laboratory, Griffith conducted extensive controls to confirm this unexpected finding. Avery's laboratory at the Rockefeller Institute pursued the question of what substance mediated transformation. After initial skepticism, Avery's team, particularly MacLeod and McCarty, systematically purified the transforming principle through the late 1930s and early 1940s. By 1944, they had identified it as deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) through chemical analysis and enzymatic studies. The lecture also describes Avery's broader contributions to pneumococcal research, including characterization of type-specific capsular polysaccharides and discovery of polysaccharide-degrading enzymes. Downie emphasizes the methodical, cautious approach both Griffith and Avery shared, and highlights how Avery's access to resources and collaborative team enabled him to extend Griffith's serendipitous discovery into a fundamental understanding of genetic material.

Key findings

  • Fred Griffith discovered in 1928 that rough avirulent pneumococci could be transformed into virulent smooth forms by exposure to heat-killed virulent cells, establishing the phenomenon of bacterial transformation.
  • Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty identified deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) as the chemical substance responsible for pneumococcal transformation through systematic purification and chemical analysis.
  • The identification of DNA as the transforming principle demonstrated that nucleic acids, not proteins, carry genetic information and hereditary traits in bacteria.
  • Avery's team developed methods to consistently prepare active transforming extracts by 1937 and used enzymatic digestion and alcohol precipitation to purify the transforming principle.
  • MacLeod's subsequent work extended transformation studies to streptococci, demonstrating genetic exchange between distantly related bacterial species.
